meta_title: For Founders: SaaS starter kit to Solve no project ideas (Intermediate Checklist)
meta_description: Learn how to SaaS starter kit and overcome no project ideas. A Intermediate-level Checklist using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: SaaS Starter Kit: Solve 'No Project Ideas' (Founder Checklist)
content.seo_description: Stop guessing. Use this intermediate SaaS starter kit checklist to validate ideas, build MVPs faster, and generate revenue using Antigravity and Claude Code.
content.answer_block: The 'no idea' trap is a failure of observation, not creativity. By leveraging a modular SaaS starter kit, you decouple the boilerplate from the business logic. Use Antigravity to scaffold production-ready architecture and Claude Code to iterate on high-value features, allowing you to validate market demand in days, not months.
content.intro:
The blank screen of a code editor is the graveyard of ambition. You’ve spent weeks circling the drain of 'what to build,' paralyzed by the fear that your next project will be another ghost town of unmonetized lines of code. The market doesn't care about your clever tech stack; it cares about the specific, painful problems you can solve for a price.

True mastery isn't about finding the 'perfect' idea; it’s about having a high-velocity system to ship, test, and pivot. By adopting a hardened SaaS starter kit, you stop reinventing authentication and payments, and start building the features that actually turn your project into a business.

steps.title: Validate the Pain Point
steps.description: Before writing code, define the 'job to be done.' Use an AI-driven framework to map a specific user struggle to a SaaS solution.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Act as a SaaS architect. Analyze the current market for 'unsolved administrative tasks for niche freelancers.' Generate three potential SaaS micro-SaaS ideas that solve these pain points with a high willingness to pay.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'Analyze market trends for niche freelancer pain points and output a prioritized list of 3 SaaS ideas.'

steps.title: Scaffold the Core Architecture
steps.description: Deploy your infrastructure using a starter kit to ensure your app has production-grade auth, billing, and database connectivity from the first commit.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Bootstrap a production-ready SaaS starter kit with Next.js, Supabase, and Stripe integration. Ensure strict type-safety and a modular folder structure for rapid feature expansion.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'Initialize new SaaS project using the boilerplate architecture provided by the starter kit.'

steps.title: Implement the 'Killer Feature'
steps.description: Build the single feature that solves the core problem. Focus on utility over aesthetics to reach MVP status immediately.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a service layer for the core feature identified in Step 1. Ensure the API handles edge cases and is ready for unit testing.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'Implement the primary business logic for the MVP feature set.'

steps.title: Integrate Monetization Logic
steps.description: Don't build a product that doesn't accept money. Connect your billing flow immediately to validate market intent.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a secure Stripe webhook handler to upgrade user subscription status in the database upon successful payment.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'Connect Stripe webhooks to the user subscription model and test the billing flow.'

steps.title: Deploy and Iterate
steps.description: Push to production and observe real usage. Use telemetry to identify where users drop off.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Configure a CI/CD pipeline for the current stack that automates deployment to Vercel/Fly.io and runs smoke tests.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'Configure automated deployment pipeline and perform a final production smoke test.'

pro_tips: Never build a custom auth system; use your starter kit's pre-configured provider to save 20+ hours of security engineering.
Focus on the 'Time to First Dollar.' If your MVP takes longer than 14 days to build, you are over-engineering.
Use Claude Code to write your unit tests as you build; it forces you to clarify your requirements before the code is finalized.
Build 'thin' wrappers around LLM APIs; the real value is in the data you collect, not the prompt engineering.
Market your product while building it; share your 'build-in-public' progress to capture early adopters before the code is even finished.
content.next_steps: Once your MVP is live, focus entirely on user acquisition and feedback loops. Use the data from your first 10 users to iterate on the product roadmap.
content.conversion_block: Stop letting your best ideas die in a text file. The gap between an amateur and a founder is the willingness to ship imperfect code. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
